evals.prompt: We're rolling out AI coding tools on our 5-year-old TypeScript monorepo. Infer our conventions and generate a steering file so AI-written code matches our style. The repo is in the current directory.
evals.expected_output: A sampled analysis (10-20 representative files) producing per-dimension conventions with observed frequencies, an under-50-line steering file containing only conventions an agent would get wrong, and inconsistencies reported separately with counts.
expectations: Samples representative files rather than claiming to read everything
Every emitted rule cites observed frequency or evidence from the codebase
Steering file is concise and excludes anything a formatter already enforces
Inconsistencies are presented with counts for team decision, not silently resolved

evals.prompt: Generate an ESLint config that matches how our JavaScript codebase is actually written - we have no lint setup at all today.
evals.expected_output: An ESLint config inferred from observed code patterns, with non-default rules commented with their evidence, after checking no conflicting config exists.
expectations: Checks for existing linter/formatter configs before generating
Non-default rules carry evidence comments (e.g. frequency counts)
Config is descriptive of current code, not an imposed external standard

evals.prompt: Our repo has only 6 small Python files, all written by different people in different styles. Extract our code standards.
evals.expected_output: An honest assessment that the codebase is too small/inconsistent to infer conventions, with what was actually observed, and a recommendation to adopt a community standard as the baseline.
expectations: Declines to fabricate conventions from insufficient evidence
Reports what was actually observed in the 6 files
Recommends a community standard baseline as the constructive path
